coredata.dc:description: Energy storage technologies enable the efficient storage and release of energy, providing essential flexibility and stability to power grids worldwide. Supercapacitors are advanced energy storage systems capable of rapidly storing and releasing large amounts of electrical energy, offering long cycle life and high-power density. Herein, a carbon quantum dot (CQD) dispersed 2D graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanocomposite was deposited with poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) by an electrodeposition technique. The structural, morphological, functional group, and elemental characteristics of the synthesized materials were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). An electrochemical study of electrode materials named g-C3N4, g-C3N4-CQD and g-C3N4-CQD/PEDOT (GCP) composites was performed. The GCP electrode-based symmetric supercapacitor device exhibited a specific capacitance (Cs) of 109.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4. Herein, the prime novelty is the incorporation of CQDs as spacers between g-C3N4 layers, which substantially improved the surface area, providing potential benefits such as higher energy density and greater stability for supercapacitors. The supercapacitor device utilizing GCP demonstrated an energy density of 14.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1.4 kW kg−1, operating at a current density of 0.2 A g−1. The improved electrochemical performance of the hybrid electrode materials is ascribed to the combined effect of the faradaic PEDOT and the non-faradaic CQDs incorporated into the g-C3N4 matrix.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride supported metal-free heterostructure embedded with carbon quantum dots and PEDOT as electrodes for supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Energy storage technologies enable the efficient storage and release of energy, providing essential flexibility and stability to power grids worldwide. Supercapacitors are advanced energy storage systems capable of rapidly storing and releasing large amounts of electrical energy, offering long cycle life and high-power density. Herein, a carbon quantum dot (CQD) dispersed 2D graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanocomposite was deposited with poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) by an electrodeposition technique. The structural, morphological, functional group, and elemental characteristics of the synthesized materials were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). An electrochemical study of electrode materials named g-C3N4, g-C3N4-CQD and g-C3N4-CQD/PEDOT (GCP) composites was performed. The GCP electrode-based symmetric supercapacitor device exhibited a specific capacitance (Cs) of 109.5 F g−1 at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4. Herein, the prime novelty is the incorporation of CQDs as spacers between g-C3N4 layers, which substantially improved the surface area, providing potential benefits such as higher energy density and greater stability for supercapacitors. The supercapacitor device utilizing GCP demonstrated an energy density of 14.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1.4 kW kg−1, operating at a current density of 0.2 A g−1. The improved electrochemical performance of the hybrid electrode materials is ascribed to the combined effect of the faradaic PEDOT and the non-faradaic CQDs incorporated into the g-C3N4 matrix.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing demands for hybrid vehicles and advanced electronic devices have necessitated better energy storage systems that offer enhanced power, extended lifespan, improved safety, and affordability [1]. Consequently, supercapacitors are becoming a promising solution in the field of energy storage. Supercapacitors (SCs) or electrochemical capacitors have gained recognition for their distinctive attributes, including high power density, rapid charging cycles, and extended cycle life, distinguishing them from conventional energy storage devices such as batteries [2]. Supercapacitors are an excellent complement to batteries, particularly for meeting high-power requirements. Their flexible and adaptable designs are suitable for wearable and portable electronic devices [3]. Additionally, their consistent performance across a wide range of temperatures makes them valuable in adverse environments, fostering sustainability by being safe and environmentally benign [4]. SCs consist of two electrodes, a separator, and an electrolyte. The electrodes are crucial in determining their performance, as they are responsible for charge storage through electrostatic or electrochemical processes. High surface area, conductivity, and stability are essential properties of electrode materials [5]. Novel electrode materials under research include metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), Mxenes, etc. These materials claim to impart tunable porosity for improved ion accessibility and exceptional stability [6,7]. For instance, Zr-MOFs constructed with Zr4+ ions and ligands derived from tetrathiafulvalene were used as anodes for lithium-ion capacitors (LICs). The Zr-MOFs exhibited high stability and hierarchical porosity, enabling efficient ion transport and uniform active site distribution. The resulting Zr-MOF//activated carbon LICs delivered impressive electrochemical performance, including a high energy density of 122.5 Wh kg⁻1, a power density of 12.5 kW kg⁻1, and a cycling stability of 86 % retention over 1000 cycles at 2000 mA g⁻1 within a voltage window of 1.0–4.0 V [8]. Similarly, Ni-MOF/PANI composite prepared via ultrasonic mixing demonstrated an enhanced specific capacity of 122 C g⁻1 and exceptional stability of 99.25 % capacitance retention (5000 cycles) compared to Ni-MOF (42 C g⁻1) and PANI (72 C g⁻1) [9].

Carbon-based materials possess benefits such as large surface area, cost-effectiveness, and availability and have traditionally been utilized as electrodes in supercapacitors [10]. Hybrid materials combining carbon with metal oxides, conducting polymers, or transition metal dichalcogenides show promise for synergistic energy density and durability improvements. A novel hybrid ternary composite, In2O3/g-C3N4/MWCNT, was developed using a one-pot solvothermal approach by integrating 3D In2O3, 2D g-C3N4, and 1D MWCNTs. The presence of In2O3 mitigates the restacking of g-C3N4 layers, increasing active surface area and contributing to pseudocapacitance, while MWCNTs act as conductive linkers, shortening ion diffusion paths. Electrochemical testing demonstrated a high specific capacitance of 1081 F g⁻1 at 1 A g⁻1 and impressive cycle stability with 97.5 % retention after 5000 cycles at 3 A g⁻1 [11]. A lithium-ion capacitor (LIC) was developed using an oxygen-deficient black TiO2₋x/graphene (B-TiO2₋x/G) aerogel as the anode and microporous carbon derived from biomass as the cathode. The incorporation of graphene nanosheets lowered the energy barrier for Li⁺ diffusion within the B-TiO2₋x structure. This LIC achieved a high energy density of 166.4 Wh kg⁻1 at 200 mA g⁻1 and a power density of 7.9 kW kg⁻1 at 17.1 Wh kg⁻1. It also retained 87 % of its capacitance after 3,000 cycles at a current density of 2,000 mA g⁻1 [12]. Recently, carbon allotropes doped with nitrogen have come to the forefront as electrode materials for supercapacitors [13,14]. Their effectiveness is attributed to a pair of electrons in the nitrogen atom, which facilitates a strong interaction with the electrolyte [15]. For example, a high-performance electrode was developed by synthesizing N-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) decorated with CoS2 nanoparticles using the hydrothermal method. The N-doping process improved the surface area and porosity of the rGO nanosheets, resulting in hierarchical porous nanostructures that enhanced ion diffusion, charge transport, and the dispersion of CoS2. As a result, the electrode delivered a capacitance of 797 F g⁻1 at 0.6 A g⁻1, with cycling stability of 86.02 % capacitance retention after 13000 cycles [16]. In a separate study, nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles (PM-CNPs) were recycled from diesel engine emissions, exhibiting a size range of 25–40 nm, an average pore size of ∼3.3 nm, and a nitrogen content of ∼1.1 at %. The PM-CNPs electrodes demonstrated a specific capacitance of 134.4 F g⁻1 at 0.1 A g⁻1 in 6 M KOH and displayed excellent stability after 5000 cycles as metal-free materials for clean energy applications [17]. g-C3N4 is a nitrogen-doped carbon allotrope comprising a 3:4 ratio of carbon (C) to nitrogen (N) (tri-s-triazine units) [18]. g-C3N4 has a layered structure in a two-dimensional (2D) configuration similar to that of sp2-hybridized N-substituted graphene [19]. Its exceptional thermal and chemical stability, chemical composition, and tunable electronic structure make it suitable for tailoring modifications to improve the specific properties of composites. Moreover, precursors for g-C3N4 are abundant, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly, making it a potential material for sustainable energy storage devices [20,21]. In a study conducted by Gonçalves et al., unmodified g-C3N4 achieved a Cs of 113.7 F g−1 with approximately 89 % maintenance after 5000 cycles, in addition to exhibiting specific energy and power values of 76.5 Wh kg−1 and 11.9 W kg−1, respectively [22]. However, the low conductivity and accumulation of bulk g-C3N4 limit its application, which can be overcome by hybridization with carbon materials, metal compounds or conducting polymers [23]. In another study, the author successfully prepared nitrogen-doped porous carbon electrodes by carbonization of protonated g-C3N4 and a polypyrrole (pg-C3N4/PPy) nanocomposite. The porous carbon derived from pg-C3N4/PPy retained a capacitance of 350 F g−1 through 1000 charge-discharge cycles [24]. Furthermore, Huang et al. developed a heterostructure Ti3C2Tx/g-C3N4 electrode for solid-state supercapacitors. It exhibited an impressive Cs of 414 F g−1 when operating at 1 A g−1 with significant energy and power densities of 23.9 Wh kg−1 and 139.6 W kg−1, respectively [25].

Conducting polymers (CPs) are well-known pseudocapacitive materials composed of organic macromolecules with the ability to transport charge carriers. This results in electrical conductivity, attributed to the presence of π-conjugated systems within their molecular structures [26]. They possess exceptional redox properties, enabling them to release charge carriers through reversible redox reactions, which makes them an ideal choice for energy storage devices such as batteries and supercapacitors [27]. However, supercapacitor electrodes consisting solely of conducting polymers have been found to lack stability, exhibit inadequate power efficiency, and have a limited cycle life [28]. Researchers have investigated composites of CPs with various carbon materials to mitigate these limitations and enhance electrode performance. PEDOT, belonging to the polythiophene family, stands out among the investigated conductive polymers due to its exceptional attributes [29]. Its high conductivity, rapid charge/discharge capabilities, broad voltage window, and environmentally benign characteristics make it a preferred choice for electrode materials [30]. Electrochemical polymerization offers a straightforward approach to acquiring PEDOT from EDOT [31]. PEDOT was the first among the conducting polymers to be embedded with g-C3N4 for supercapacitors. The PEDOT/graphite-phase C3N4 composite synthesized by the layer-by-layer assembly method exhibited a Cs of 200 F g−1 in Na2SO4 and 137 F g−1 in H2SO4 at a current density of 2 A g−1. Notably, Na2SO4 (compared to H2SO4) significantly enhances power and energy density [32]. Similarly, Xu et al. synthesized a PEDOT/g-C3N4 composite by a direct mixing technique. This electrode demonstrated a peak Cs of 277 F g−1 (at 1 A g−1) in 1 M H2SO4 while preserving approximately 94 % of its original capacitance after 5000 cycles [33].

Carbon materials with different dimensions, such as activated carbon, 1D CNTs, 2D graphene, 3D porous carbon foams, etc., have been used for energy storage devices such as batteries and supercapacitors [34–36]. The carbon layer acts as a buffer during charging and discharging, improving the conductivity and dispersion of active materials for effective energy storage [37]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon materials with unique physicochemical properties that have garnered significant attention in recent years. CQDs, which are typically less than 10 nanometers in size, exhibit excellent electrochemical behaviour owing to their large surface area, abundant active sites, and tunable surface functionalities [38]. CQDs possess remarkable photoluminescence properties, biocompatibility, easy synthesis methods and cost-effectiveness, which have contributed to their widespread utilization in diverse fields, including photocatalysis, bioimaging, sensors, and energy conversion and storage systems [39]. Carbon-coated g-C3N4 nanotubes were prepared by the calcination of urea at 550 °C for use in supercapacitors. The Cs of g-C3N4 increased to 241 F g−1 from 124.4 F g−1 when it was coated with carbon, as observed at 1 A g−1 in 1 M KOH [40]. Yang et al. synthesized composite electrodes using NSAC-PANI, which combines highly conductive dual-element-doped (N and S) activated carbon and PANI with a high theoretical capacitance. The binary electrode NSAC-PANI displayed a Cs of approximately 300 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4. The energy and power densities of the symmetric device were in the range of 20−25 Wh kg−1 and 380–400 W kg−1, respectively, and the retention rate was 90 % (5,000 cycles) [41]. The use of N-doped carbon (N-C) and Polydopamine (PDA) effectively stabilized Ge-based anode materials. PDA prevented GeO2 nanoparticle reaggregation, and its N-doped carbon ensured uniform dispersion of Ge nanoparticles, resulting in Ge/N-C composites exhibiting high discharge capacity (981 mA h g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 after 100 cycles, with 74 % capacity retention) and excellent lithium storage performance in lithium-ion batteries [42]. However, limited literature has been reported on the electrochemical behaviour of g-C3N4 and CQDs across different applications.

In the present study, a ternary hybrid g-C3N4-CQD/PEDOT (GCP) electrode material was synthesized by anchoring CQDs and depositing pseudocapacitive PEDOT on 2D g-C3N4 by an electrodeposition method (Fig. 1 ). Previous studies on g-C3N4 highlighted limitations such as limited surface area and inadequate electrical conductivity, which hindered its direct use as an electrode material in supercapacitors. To overcome these challenges, CQDs were introduced as fillers, effectively preventing the aggregation of g-C3N4 layers and exposing additional surface-active sites. These active sites in the g-C3N4-CQD composite served as nucleation points for PEDOT growth. The addition of PEDOT significantly enhanced the electrical conductivity of the GCP composite. The resulting ternary GCP hybrid material was used as an electrode in symmetric-type supercapacitors. This study promotes SDG 7 (Affordable and clean energy). This study aimed to examine the electrochemical characteristics of modified GCP electrodes with the role of CQD for the first time in a symmetric two-electrode system. The current study will be a valuable reference for researchers developing economically benign, environmentally sustainable and high-efficiency electrode materials for energy storage.

The materials used in the study included urea (Loba Chemie), poly (3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) (Aldrich), polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) (Sigma‒Aldrich), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (Sigma‒Aldrich), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) (Fisher Scientific). Ultrapure water was used to prepare the solutions.

The synthesis of CQDs was similar to that of previously reported hydrothermal methods [43,44]. Typically, 60 mg of starch was mixed with 30 ml of deionized water, stirred for 15 min and ultrasonicated for another 15 min. The blend was placed into a stainless-steel autoclave vessel lined with 50 ml of polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 °C for 8 h in a muffle furnace. The resulting precipitate was isolated by centrifugation at 5,000 rpm for approximately 20 min. The solution was then filtered through a 0.22 µm polyethersulfone (PES) filter membrane and dialysed against ultrapure water through a cellulose dialysis membrane (Mw=1,000 Da) for 24 h. The resultant solution was brown in colour with dispersed CQDs. g-C3N4 was synthesized using urea (CH4N2O) as a precursor by a modified calcination method [45]. This method involved heating 20 g of urea in a crucible to 550 °C at a 5 °C/min rate for 4 h in a muffle furnace. The resulting pale-yellow powder was finely ground using an agate mortar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the GCP electrode and symmetric supercapacitor device
ce:section.ce:para: The g-C3N4-CQD nanocomposite was prepared by blending 50 mg of g-C3N4 with 50 ml CQD dispersion (1:1 weight ratio), followed by heating and drying. This g-C3N4-CQD (20 mg) composite was then combined with PVDF and NMP at a ratio of 8:1:1 [46,47] and coated onto a stainless steel substrate of 1 cm2 area. The GCP electrode was prepared by cyclic voltametric method using EDOT in the range of 0.1 mg to 0.5 mg with the presence of sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) surfactant (0.01 mg) in 1 M H2SO4 solution. The CV was performed using g-C3N4-CQD as working electrode, platinum electrode as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode in the range of -0.2 V to +0.8 V at 50 mV s−1. The optimized amount of EDOT was 0.5 mg as it produced uniform coating within 50 cycles. While one-step methods are more straightforward and faster, CV offers better control over film properties like morphology and thickness, which was critical for this study. CV was chosen to optimize these parameters, but one-step methods remain a viable alternative depending on the application. A symmetric supercapacitor device was then prepared using the GCP electrodes and 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte.

An X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku) was used to analyze the crystalline structure and phase development of the prepared materials. Infrared-ray spectra of the materials were recorded with a Shimadzu FTIR spectrophotometer to characterize the functional groups of the composites. The surface morphologies of the materials were analyzed using a Carl-Zeiss SEM-EDAX instrument and the FEI Tecnai G2 F30 TEM instrument. Axis Supra XPS (Kratos Analytical Ltd.) was used to analyze the surface chemistry of the materials, employing an Al Ka X-ray source. UV‒Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800) and spectrofluorometer by Shimadzu were utilized for quantitative and qualitative analysis of the CQDs.

The electrochemical characterization involved the utilization of a Biologic SP-50e electrochemical workstation. These measurements were carried out on three materials, g-C3N4, g-C3N4-CQD, and GCP, using a two-electrode configuration in a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution. In this configuration, a platinum wire was used as both the counter and reference electrodes, while the prepared g-C3N4 composites were used as the working electrode. The electrochemical tests included three main techniques: cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analyses. The CV analysis involved cycling potential changes within the range of -0.2 V to 0.8 V, utilizing different scan rates varying from 5 to 100 mV s−1. GCD assessments were performed at current densities ranging between 0.2 A g−1 and 1 A g−1. The EIS experiments used a frequency range of 1 MHz−100 mHz, with an applied amplitude of 50 mV. The following equations are used to calculate the specific capacitance (Cs), energy density (E), and power density (P) for the electrodes from the GCD curves [48].

where I (A), m (g), Δt (s), and ΔV (V) denote the charge-discharge current, active mass of electrode material, discharge time, and potential window, respectively.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a) were analyzed to identify phase formation in the g-C3N4 composites. In the g-C3N4 spectrum, diffraction peaks at 27.5° correspond to the (002) plane, resembling a hexagonal phase [49]. In the spectrum of g-C3N4-CQDs, a significant diffraction peak at 27.7° signifies the presence of graphitic aromatic C–N units [50]. This indicates the successful incorporation of CQDs and the introduction of defects within the matrix of g-C3N4 [51]. Furthermore, the g-C3N4-CQD composite also exhibited several additional peaks, reflecting the substantial content of CQDs within the composite material [52]. The GCP composite spectrum has a peak at 22.4°, indicating the presence of PEDOT. Additionally, the peaks at 15° and 18° indicate the spacing between two stacks within the two-dimensional stacking pattern of the polymer chains and the conciliating g-C3N4-CQDs [53]. The interaction between the aggregated g-C3N4-CQDs and the PEDOT polymer chains disrupted the crystalline regions, indicating phase formation between the components of the GCP composite.

FTIR analysis (Fig. 2b) was performed on the samples in the 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumber range to examine the phase formation of the individual components and to identify the functional groups on the surface of the materials. The FTIR spectra of g-C3N4, g-C3N4-CQD, and GCP exhibited typical peaks attributed to the g-C3N4 phase. In the g-C3N4 pattern, the peak at 808 cm−1 corresponds to the breathing vibration of triazine rings, a key structural unit of g-C3N4. This vibrational mode involves symmetric in-and-out movement of atoms within the triazine rings, confirming the presence of these aromatic units [51]. The peaks present in the 1001–1110 cm−1 frequency range correspond to the bending vibrations related to –OH functional groups, while the peaks in the 1200–1600 cm−1 range are attributed to the –CN stretching modes found in the heterocyclic rings of s-triazine units. Additionally, the broad bands in the range of 2500-3000 cm−1 and 3000-3300 cm−1 represented the stretching N–H bonds and stretching vibrations of adsorbed H2O molecules, respectively, [54]. In the spectrum of the g-C3N4-CQD composite, several peaks were observed in the 808 cm−1 to 1630 cm−1 range. These peaks are attributed to vibrations such as bending C–H bonds and stretching C–O–C or C=C bonds resulting from the precursor (starch), which stabilize the CQDs and contribute to their chemical and optical properties [55]. In the GCP composite spectrum, the presence of PEDOT on the surface can be confirmed by the characteristic peaks between 808 cm−1 and 1330 cm−1, which correspond to S-O and C-S bonds [56].

The UV‒vis absorbance spectrum (Fig. 2c) of the CQDs in the near-infrared region (200–800 nm) was analyzed to determine the concentration of the CQD compound. The concentration of a CQD is proportional to the amount of light it absorbs at a specific wavelength. The prepared carbon quantum dots showed a characteristic absorbance at 255 nm in the 200–350 nm wavelength range, corresponding to the concentrated CQDs in the sample [57]. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 2d) were analyzed to confirm the formation of the as-prepared CQDs. The CQDs display broad emission spectra that cover a span of wavelengths extending from the ultraviolet to the near-infrared region. They show narrower emission peak widths when exposed to various excitation wavelengths ranging from 400–480 nm. The maximum emission peak occurs at 500 nm at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm, which demonstrates the presence of distinct emissive states within the CQDs [57]. As the excitation wavelength increases, a redshift is observed in the emission peaks of the CQDs, meaning the emitted light shifts toward longer wavelengths in the spectrum. Notably, these emission peaks remain symmetrical along the X-axis, indicating that the shape of the emission profile does not distort significantly with the change in excitation wavelength. This intriguing behaviour is likely a result of various factors, including the varying cluster size of the carbon dot due to agglomeration, as large clusters tend to emit light at longer wavelengths due to quantum confinement phenomenon. or the existence of different functional groups superficial to the carbon dots, which can affect their interaction with light and consequently alter the emission characteristics [58]. As the excitation wavelength increases, a redshift is observed in the emission peaks of the carbon dots, meaning the emitted light shifts toward longer wavelengths in the spectrum. Notably, these emission peaks remain symmetrical along the X-axis, indicating that the shape of the emission profile does not distort significantly with the change in excitation wavelength. This intriguing behaviour is likely influenced by several factors inherent to the carbon dots. One possible explanation is the variation in the size of the carbon dots, as larger dots tend to emit light at longer wavelengths due to quantum confinement effects. Another contributing factor could be the presence of different functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots, which can affect their interaction with light and consequently alter the emission characteristics. Together, these factors underscore the unique optical properties of carbon dots, making them highly versatile for applications requiring tunable fluorescence.

SEM was used to analyze the surface morphology of materials. The g-C3N4 sample exhibited an irregular flake-like structure, as shown in Fig. 3 a. This morphology has been described in the literature as characteristic of g-C3N4 nanosheets [59], which serve as an effective substrate for attaching CQDs. Following the integration of CQDs, the g-C3N4-CQDs composite demonstrated a denser and more clustered nanostructure, distinct from the morphology of pure g-C3N4, as depicted in Fig. 3b. Such structural changes have been similarly reported in previous studies [60].

Numerous π-bonds in both PEDOT and g-C3N4 allow for the effective integration of g-C3N4 into a PEDOT matrix facilitated by π-π solid interactions [61]. These interactions may lead to phase segregation of the PEDOT chains, resulting in the shaping of grains on the surface of the GCP composite and resulting in a coagulated coral-like morphology, as shown in Fig. 3c. These dispersed structural arrangements can reduce the diffusion path for the electrolyte, thereby enhancing its suitability for charge storage. EDS was integrated with SEM to analyze the elemental mapping and space distribution within the composite material. EDS data were collected through area analysis, showing the peaks of nitrogen and carbon, indicating the existence of both pristine g-C3N4 and the CQDs. Additionally, signals corresponding to sulfur (S) and oxygen (O) were detected, confirming the presence of PEDOT [53]. The presence of fluorine (F) is attributed to the use of PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) as a binder in the composite. PVDF is commonly employed due to its chemical stability and strong adhesive properties. The percentage of the composition of elements is shown in the inset table (Fig. 3d).

The HR-TEM images showed multilayered g-C3N4/CQD, where 2D g-C3N4 was embedded with uniformly distributed CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 a. Upon magnification, the presence of numerous CQDs was observed on the underlying g-C3N4, creating a microscopic heterojunction between CQDs and g-C3N4 (Fig. 4b). An evenly dispersed CQDs across the 2D surface of g-C3N4 indicates its strong holding capacity for CQDs, consistent with findings in the literature [46]. When carbon QDs are further magnified, the lattice fringe spacing measures 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphite, as seen in Fig. 4(c and d). The TEM images show that the CQDs are almost spherical in shape, and the size distribution ranges from 1.95 nm to 8.8 nm with an average size of 4.63 nm [62], as shown in Fig. 4e. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of g-C3N4/CQD showed moderate crystallinity due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups within the CQDs (Fig. 4f) as per the literature [63].

XPS analysis (Fig. 5 ) was used to determine the electronic structure and chemical constituents of the samples. The survey spectrum and scanned spectra for O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p were recorded for the GCP composite material. The survey spectrum (Fig. 5e) validates the existence of constituent phases within the composite material. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 5a) of GCP reveals three distinct components upon deconvolution: oxygen atoms in carboxyl groups (532.3 eV), adsorbed oxygen atoms in H2O, and oxygen atoms in esters and anhydrides (531.7 eV and 531.2 eV, respectively), attributed to the existence of g-C3N4-CQDs and PEDOT in the composite [64]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5b) exhibits three distinct peaks corresponding to C=N-C groups (397.3 eV), N-(C)3 groups (399.2 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV) [65]. The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) revealed three primary peaks located at 284.5 eV, 286 eV, and 287.6 eV corresponding to sp2-hybridized C=C/C-C, C-S arising from the PEDOT and carbon interface, and N-C=N associated with the triazine/heptazine units of g-C3N4, respectively [66]. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 5d) shows doublet spin-orbit peaks at 168.4 eV and 169.5 eV attributed to the nitrogen (N) in g-C3N4, which likely donates electrons to the sulfur in PEDOT, establishing a stable composite structure between g-C3N4 and PEDOT. Additionally, there is another doublet at 165 eV and 164.6 eV (S 2p1/2) and a peak at 163.5 eV (S 2p3/2) [33].

Fig. 6 (a), (b) and (c) show the cyclic voltammetry (CV) profiles of the g-C3N4, g-C3N4-CQD, and GCP electrodes, respectively. The cyclic voltammetry measurements of the three electrodes were conducted at scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mV s−1, all within the potential window of −0.2 V−0.8 V. The CV profile obtained from the test indicated that the GCP electrode exhibited pseudocapacitive behavior with a significantly larger area under the curve compared to the g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-CQD electrodes as depicted in Fig. 6d. Additionally, a substantial increase in current for the GCP electrode compared to the individual materials indicates improved electrochemical behaviour. These results imply that a combined effect among g-C3N4, CQD, and PEDOT enhances the supercapacitance performance of composite GCP electrodes. Furthermore, with an increase in scan rate, the voltammograms of the device retain their uniform shape with minimal deviation, indicating that the electrode materials exhibit high conductivity and stability [67]. The Cs values from the CV profiles for the g-C3N4-CQD and GCP electrodes at a 5 mV s−1 scan rate were calculated to be 181 F g−1 and 447 F g−1, respectively, which exceeded that of basic g-C3N4 (166.7 F g−1). Furthermore, the variation in Cs with scan rate (mV s−1) for the three electrodes is shown in Fig. 6e. The specific capacitance decreases as the scan rate increases, indicating more interaction time at lower scan rates [68]. The composite g-C3N4-CQD and GCP electrodes demonstrated excellent electrochemical performance associated with basic g-C3N4, ascribed to the capacitive contribution from the CQDs and PEDOT. Although incorporation of CQD into g-C3N4 was not able to produce large current difference but its conductivity increased as observed in EIS studies. Fig. 7 shows Nyquist plots of all the three electrodes wherein GCP electrode had shown the least resistance (Rct=9 Ω) followed by g-C3N4-CQD (Rct=11 Ω) and g-C3N4 (Rct=19 Ω) electrodes respectively. This indicates that the CQD promotes movement of ions between the g-C3N4 layers.

CV analysis of the GCP symmetric supercapacitor device in Fig. 8 a showed hybrid capacitive behaviour at scan rates varying between 5−100 mV s−1 within a voltage difference of –0.2 V to 0.8 V. A substantial increase in current for the GCP device compared to the electrodes indicates improved electrochemical behaviour. EIS measurements were conducted to further analyze the performance of the supercapacitor device. The Nyquist plot of the GCP device Fig. 8b displays a distinctive semicircle pattern in the low-frequency bandwidth and a vertical line in the high-frequency bandwidth. The Rct value associated with the GCP device was measured to be 54 Ω, indicating enhanced conductivity and capacitance properties. The smaller the arc radius is, the lower the resistance at the interface of the electrode and electrolyte. Furthermore, a low Rct indicates reduced Warburg resistance but superior capacitive performance at lower frequencies [69]. The high-frequency bandwidth of the Nyquist plot correlates with ionic diffusion to the electrode surface from the electrolyte solution. The plot of the GCP device showed an almost vertical line parallel to the y-axis, which is an ideal condition for supercapacitors. Additionally, a shorter length of the vertical line, indicating the minimal imaginary part of the impedance, signifies a low resistance to diffusion of ions from the electrolyte solution [70].

The GCD measurement profiles for the supercapacitor device at various current densities from 0.2 A g−1 to 1 A g−1 (specifically 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1) in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte are depicted in Fig. 8c. The symmetric GCP supercapacitor device exhibited almost triangular-shaped GCD curves, which indicates excellent efficiency for the charge‒discharge process. The initial IR drop in the GCD profile shows the internal resistance of the electrode materials due to the bulk solution resistance, electrical contact resistance, and ion migration resistance [71]. The decrease in the IR (voltage) of the device is considerably lower (∼0.02 V), indicating the exceptional charge efficiency of the device (GCP). Furthermore, the Cs values of the device were determined from the GCD curves using Eq. (1). The Cs of the GCP device at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 was found to be 109.5 F g−1. The capacitance of the device decreases at high current densities due to the restricted ionic interaction at the interface of the electrode and electrolyte. At higher current densities, the rapid charge/discharge process limits the time available for ions in the electrolyte to effectively interact with the electrode surface. This results in incomplete utilization of the active material, leading to a reduction in the capacitance [72]. The specific energy density and specific power density of the device were determined from the GCD profiles using Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively. At 0.2 A g−1, the GCP symmetric device achieved an energy density of 14.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1.4 kW kg−1. The improved Cs may be ascribed to the synergistic support of g-C3N4-CQDs and PEDOT, where PEDOT potentially improves ion transport through delocalized π-π electrons [73]. Examining the GCD curves of the composites also reveals that the discharge time increases with decreasing current density. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the more efficient utilization of electroactive materials at low current densities [74]. At high current densities, the movement of ions in the electrolyte through diffusion into the innermost areas of the electroactive materials is hindered, subsequently affecting the performance of the supercapacitor [75]. The stacked charge-discharge (GCD) curves of the composite supercapacitor Fig. 8d at varying current densities ranging from 0.2 A g−1 to 1 A g−1 are presented. Fig. 8e depicts the charge distribution controlled by diffusion and capacitance at various scan rates; as the scan rate increased, the corresponding capacitive-controlled charge ratio also increased. The results indicate that the ratio of diffusion-controlled charge exceeds the capacitive-controlled charge ratio for GCP electrodes. This is attributed to the combination of g-C3N4 and CQD with conductive PEDOT, which enhances redox reactions, leading to ion diffusion into the bulk electrode material, thereby increasing hybrid capacitance [37]. The synergistic role of PEDOT and CQD is that the PEDOT acts as a coating on the surface of the electrode, providing a capacitive property wherein the ions penetrate this layer. The CQD embedded in g-C3N4 attracts these ions to diffuse through g-C3N4 layers. Hence, they provide a channel for ion movement and storage together. The composite retained approximately 82.32 % of its original capacitance after 5000 cycles, indicating good cyclic stability and a stable channel for ionic movements (Fig. 8f).

Scheme 1 shows the probable mechanism depicting the charge/discharge process in the supercapacitor. The electrodeposition of PEDOT was optimized to 50 CV cycles, as exceeding this limit caused the deposited PEDOT on the electrode surface to become unstable and gradually dissolved into the solvent. A stable PEDOT surface, however, offers active sites for ionic interactions. The CQDs with an average size distribution of approximately 4.63 nm prompting the selection of 1:1 ratio of g-C3N4 to CQD. The inclusion of CQDs decreased the resistance of the solution as observed in EIS studies thereby enhancing overall electrochemical performance. The composite GCP was used as a symmetric supercapacitor. Considering that C3N4 has a graphitic multilayer structure [19], it possesses porosity and crystallinity, as confirmed by XRD and SEM. The interlayers of g-C3N4 have been embedded with CQDs, which boost connectivity and act as fillers. Furthermore, the electrodeposited PEDOT appears cloudy throughout the surface, and its uniform coating promotes redox behaviour, while the g-C3N4-CQDs promote EDLC behaviour. Hence, pseudocapacitive behaviour was observed in the CV and GCD data. As observed, PEDOT is a ‘p’ type that, during charging/oxidation, loses 2H+ ions to become bipolaron, and during discharging/reduction, it turns into a doubly bonded PEDOT. Moreover, the presence of H+ ions in the electrolyte results in a high degree of continuous accessibility through the PEDOT and g-C3N4-CQD layers toward the electrode surface. Overall, the synergistic effect of the GCP composite electrode led to high performance in the supercapacitor.

In summary, two-dimensional g-C3N4 nanosheets were embedded with CQDs and deposited with pseudocapacitive PEDOT using an electrodeposition method. The composite electrode material GCP was used in symmetric supercapacitors. The composition of g-C3N4 with CQDs and PEDOT significantly improved the electrochemical performance of g-C3N4. The specific capacitance was 166.7 F g−1 for g-C3N4, 181 F g−1 for g-C3N4-CQD, and 447 F g−1 for the GCP electrodes. The enhanced electrochemical characteristics of the prepared ternary composites can be attributed to the combined effect of faradaic PEDOT and zero-dimensional CQDs in g-C3N4. The GCP symmetric device exhibited a specific capacitance of 109.5 F g−1, with an energy density of 14.6 Wh kg−1 at 1.4 kW kg−1 power density operating at 0.2 A g−1 density of current. The GCP nanocomposite demonstrates exceptional supercapacitor features, making it a potential candidate for metal-free supercapacitors. This study provides guidance for the design and composition of high-efficiency supercapacitors and other energy storage devices. Table 1 , Eq. (1)-(3)
